findings.reachability: Any authenticated WordPress user who reaches an admin-accessible URL satisfying the show_strategy GET conditions (e.g., a subscriber who navigates to a crafted admin URL). The admin_footer hook fires for all admin pages with no role guard.
findings.justification: render_chat() gates only on show_strategy->shouldDisplay() which evaluates $_GET parameters (GetStrategy), never calls current_user_can(); any authenticated user reaching a qualifying admin URL receives the chat widget HTML including a valid nonce, leaking plugin internals to low-privilege roles.

detection_pattern.description: Finds admin_footer hook registrations that lack a current_user_can() guard in the same file; admin_footer fires for any authenticated user reaching an admin page, so handlers rendering privileged UI without a capability check expose that UI to low-privilege roles.

findings.reachability: Any user who can trigger the AJAX action (authenticated admin-level, guarded by check_ajax_referer) can supply an arbitrary instance_id value that, after sanitize_key(), is passed as a parameter to apply_filters(). Impact depends entirely on what plugins or themes hook octolize_docs_chat_settings_<slug>.
findings.justification: instance_id is read from $_GET in render_chat() and from $_POST in get_settings_ajax(), sanitized with sanitize_key() (reducing to [a-z0-9_-]), then passed to apply_filters() as part of the $data array; any registered filter receiving $data['instance_id'] without additional validation becomes an extension point for attacker-controlled input, violating the principle that filter arguments should be treated as untrusted.

detection_pattern.section_name: User input passed directly to apply_filters
detection_pattern.description: Detects apply_filters() calls where a superglobal ($_GET/$_POST/$_REQUEST) value is passed as a filter argument without apparent sanitization immediately before the call; downstream filter callbacks receiving unsanitized user input may perform unsafe operations.
